coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of erbium ions doped BiOBr via a reactive ionic liquid with improved photocatalytic activity
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Xia, Jiexiang
Ji, Mengxia
Li, Weibing
Di, Jun
Xu, Hui
He, Minqiang
Zhang, Qi
Li, Huaming
coredata.dc:description: Erbium (Er)-doped BiOBr with different Er ions doping concentrations has been synthesized through a solvothermal method in the presence of reactive ionic liquid 1-hexadecyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide ([C16mim]Br). The as-prepared samples were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier transform spectrophotometer (FT-IR), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) and UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS). The photocatalytic activities of the Er ions doped BiOBr samples were evaluated by the degradation of ciprofloxacin (CIP) under visible-light irradiation. The results assumed that doping of Er ions over BiOBr led to the increase of the photocatalytic activity under visible light irradiation and 3wt% Er/BiOBr showed the highest photocatalytic activity. The enhanced photocatalytic performance could be attributed to the improved separation efficiency of electron–hole pairs and reduced band gap.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ionic liquids
BiOBr
Er
Photocatalytic
Ciprofloxacin
Visible light

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The XRD pattern of the Er ions doped BiOBr samples and pure BiOBr synthesized by ionic liquids solvothermal treatment at 160°C for 24h.
XPS spectra of the as-prepared 3wt% Er/BiOBr composite. (a) Survey spectrum; (b) Bi 4f; (c) Br 4d; (d) Er 3d; (e) O 1s.
SEM images of the 3wt% Er/BiOBr samples. (a) The low magnification SEM image; (b) the high magnification SEM image; (c) EDS of the 3wt% Er/BiOBr microspheres structures.
Typical SEM image of 3wt% Er/BiOBr materials and corresponding elemental mapping images of Er, Bi, O and Br.
(a) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of the as‐prepared samples and (b) (αE photon)1/2 vs. Ephoton curves of the pure BiOBr and 3wt% Er/BiOBr sample.
Transient photocurrent of pure BiOBr and 3wt% Er/BiOBr samples under visible light irradiation in 0.1mol L−1 PBS solution (pH 7).
Photocatalytic degradation experiment of CIP in the presence of pure BiOBr, 1wt% Er/BiOBr, 3wt% Er/BiOBr, 5wt% Er/BiOBr and 8wt% Er/BiOBr under visible light irradiation.
Temporal UV–vis absorption spectral changes during the photocatalytic degradation of CIP in aqueous solution in the presence of 3wt% Er/BiOBr materials.
Illumination of the proposed photocatalytic mechanism.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of erbium ions doped BiOBr via a reactive ionic liquid with improved photocatalytic activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute for Energy Research, Jiangsu University, 301 Xuefu Road, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of the Environment, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Erbium ions doped BiOBr has been synthesized through a solvothermal method in the presence of reactable ionic liquid 1-hexadecyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide ([C16mim]Br). The sphere-like Er/BiOBr hierarchical structures were formed with an average diameter of 2–3μm. The photocatalytic activities of pure BiOBr and Er/BiOBr samples were evaluated by the degradation of ciprofloxacin (CIP) under visible-light irradiation, in which 3 wt% Er/BiOBr showed the highest photocatalytic activity. The enhanced photocatalytic performance could be attributed to the improved separation efficiency of electron–hole pairs and reduced band gap.

Erbium (Er)-doped BiOBr with different Er ions doping concentrations has been synthesized through a solvothermal method in the presence of reactive ionic liquid 1-hexadecyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide ([C16mim]Br). The as-prepared samples were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier transform spectrophotometer (FT-IR), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) and UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS). The photocatalytic activities of the Er ions doped BiOBr samples were evaluated by the degradation of ciprofloxacin (CIP) under visible-light irradiation. The results assumed that doping of Er ions over BiOBr led to the increase of the photocatalytic activity under visible light irradiation and 3wt% Er/BiOBr showed the highest photocatalytic activity. The enhanced photocatalytic performance could be attributed to the improved separation efficiency of electron–hole pairs and reduced band gap.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the semiconductor photocatalysts have showed great potential applications in elimination of organic pollutants [1–3]. TiO2 as a semiconductor photocatalyst was in-depth studied due to its high efficiency, good stability and nontoxicity [4,5]. However, the pure traditional TiO2 shows the low efficiency in utilizing of sunlight [6]. In this sense, recent research is focused on the development of novel alternative materials to replace traditional TiO2 capable of using more solar energy as green energy resources [7,8].

Bismuth oxybromide (BiOBr) was found to be a promising material for organic contaminants decomposition under visible-light irradiation [9,10]. The unique layered structure feature with [Bi2O2] slabs interlacing with double halogen slabs result in the production of self-built internal static electric fields in BiOBr. However, the photocatalytic activity of pure BiOBr is still limited by the high recombination of photogenerated electron–hole pairs. In this sense, many studies have committed to modify pure BiOBr [11,12]. Wang et al. [13] synthesized a novel four-leaf clover-shape BiOBr microstructure by EDTA-modified hydrothermal method. And the as-synthesized BiOBr sample exhibited excellent photocatalytic activity for the degradation of rhodamine B (RhB) in aqueous solution under visible-light irradiation. It shows that the control of morphology can improve the photocatalytic activity. Li et al. [14] reported a facile hydrothermal route to prepare BiOBr–Bi2WO6 mesoporous nanosheet composites in the presence of titanium isopropoxide. The BiOBr coupled with Bi2WO6 have high surface areas, increased light harvesting and enhanced electron–hole separation, thus exhibited high photocatalytic efficiency. The result indicates that BiOBr compound with other semiconductors could show the enhanced photocatalytic efficiency. Jiang et al. [15,16] indicated that metal ion-doping to the light-excited semiconductors such as Ti-doped BiOBr and Fe-doped BiOBr could enhance the photoabsorption and interfacial charge transfer. In these cases, the electron–hole separation efficiency and photocatalytic activity can be improved.

Rare earth elements have drawn more and more attention in the application of photocatalysis [17–19]. Doping of rare earth ions into the host semiconductors can impact its stability, phase and photocatalytic activity. For example, Cao et al. [20] synthesized a series of Sm3+-doped TiO2 and the result showed that the Sm3+ ions doping can not only suppress the phase transition from anatase to rutile of TiO2, but also inhibit the growth of TiO2 nanoparticles. The rare earth ions doped BiVO4 has also been reported, Sergio Obregón et al. [21] reported that Er doped BiVO4 exhibited enhanced photocatalytic activity for the degradation of methylene blue. It suggests that doping rare earth ions plays an important role in photocatalysis. Inspired by this, we aim to synthetize a novel erbium-doped BiOBr semiconductor so as to achieve the enhanced photocatalytic activity.

Ionic liquids (ILs) are non-volatile and non-flammable organic salts. Recently, ILs have attracted a great interest in the preparation of inorganic materials due to their high ionic conductivity, good thermal stability, good dissolving ability, wide electrochemical window, nonflammability and negligible vapor pressure [22,23]. In our previous work, we have synthesized a series of BiOX (X=Cl, Br, I) composites with reactive ionic liquids by a solvothermal process [24–26]. The result showed that ILs played important roles of solvent, reactant and template at the same time. Inspired by this, we expect that the Er-doped BiOBr will have enhanced photocatalytic activity if the Er-doped BiOBr can be controllable synthesized in the presence of ionic liquids.

Herein, we demonstrated a serious of Er ions doped BiOBr photocatalysts via a facile one-pot solvothermal method in the presence of reactive ILs [C16mim]Br. The ILs played important roles in solvent, reactant and template at the same time during the reactive process. The photocatalytic activities of Er-doped BiOBr composites were tested by the degradation of ciprofloxacin (CIP) under visible-light irradiation, and the Er-doped BiOBr showed enhanced photocatalytic activity compared with the pure BiOBr. Further more, a possible mechanism for the photocatalytic activity of Er-BiOBr on the enhancement of visible light performance was proposed.

All chemicals were analytical grade and used as received without purification. The ILs 1-hexadecyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide ([C16mim]Br) (99%) was purchased from Shanghai Chengjie Chemical Co., Ltd.

In a typical procedure (in Fig. S1.), 1mmol of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was dissolved into 20mL 2-methoxyethanol solution containing 1mmol [C16mim]Br, and then erbium nitrate (Er(NO3)3·5H2O) as a source of Er was added into the solution. The concentration of erbium ions was chosen as 1wt%, 3wt%, 5wt% and 8wt%, in which the wt% was the corresponding mass percent of as-prepared BiOBr. Furthermore, the wt% in this system was represented the original addition content of Er element. The mixture was stirred for 30min, and then transferred into 25mL Teflon-lined autoclave up to 80% of the total volume. The autoclave was then heated at 160°C for 24h and cooled down to room temperature naturally. The final product was separated by centrifugation, washed with distilled water and absolute ethanol for four times, respectively, and dried at 50°C for 24h before further characterizations. Pure BiOBr were also prepared under the same conditions. The as-obtained samples were named as x wt% Er/BiOBr, where x represents the content of Er ions.

X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) analysis was carried out on a Bruker D8 diffractometer with high-intensity Cu Kα (λ =1.54Å). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed on a PHI5300 with a monochromatic Mg Kα source to explore the elements on the surface. The field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) measurements were carried out with a field-emission scanning electron microscope (JEOL JSM-7001F) equipped with an energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscope (EDS) operated at an acceleration voltage of 10kV. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) result was carried out on an UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Corporation, Japan) using BaSO4 as a reference. The structural information for samples was measured by Fourier transform spectrophotometer (FT-IR, Avatar 470, Thermo Nicolet) using the standard KBr disk method.

The pure BiOBr and 3wt% Er/BiOBr materials modified electrodes were prepared to investigate the transition of photogenerated electrons in pure BiOBr sample and 3wt% Er/BiOBr sample. Photocurrent test systems were composed of an electrochemical working station (CHI660B, Chen Hua Instruments, Shanghai, China), a 500W Xenon lamp, and a standard three-electrode configuration. 2mg as-prepared materials was dispersed in 200μL ethanol and 200μL ethylene glycol, and then 20μL of the suspension was spread on a indium-tin oxide (ITO) glass electrode with a fixed area of 0.5×1cm2. Then the modified electrodes were dried under the infrared lamp irradiation. The ITO glass coated with as-prepared samples was used as a working electrode, a Pt wire was used as the counter electrode, and a Ag/AgCl electrode was used as the reference electrode. The electrolyte solution was 0.1M PBS solution (pH 7). All the experiments were carried out under ambient conditions.

The photocatalytic activity of the as-prepared samples was evaluated by photocatalytic degradation of Ciprofloxacin (CIP) under visible light irradiation. Experiments were carried out in a Pyrex photocatalytic reactor with a 300W Xe lamp with a UV cutoff filter (λ >400nm) as the visiblelight source, and a circulating water system to prevent thermal catalytic effects. Aeration was performed using an air pump to make sure a constant supply of oxygen and to completely mix the solution with photocatalysts during photoreactions. In a typical photocatalytic experiment, 0.01g of 3wt% Er/BiOBr powders was dispersed into 100mL CIP (10mgL−1) solution. Prior to irradiation, the solution was stirred magnetically in the dark for 30min in order to reach the adsorption equilibrium between the photocatalyst and the CIP. During irradiation, about 3mL of the suspension was continually taken from the reaction cell at intervals. The photocatalyst powder and the CIP solution were separated by centrifuging. The CIP concentration was analyzed through a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV-2450) by checking the absorbance at 276nm.

The purity and crystallinity of the as-prepared samples were examined using powder XRD measurements. Fig. 1 shows the XRD patterns of the as-prepared Er/BiOBr and pure BiOBr hierarchical architectures synthesized with ionic liquids [C16mim]Br in 2-methoxyethanol. All the diffraction peaks for the samples are readily indexed to the structure of the tetragonal phase of BiOBr (JCPDS card No. 73-2061). The average crystallite size of the nanoparticles was estimated to be about 7nm in the aggregated BiOBr microspheres by the Scherrer's Equation: D = Kλ/βcosθ, where D was the mean diameter of the crystallite, K was a constant related to the dimensionless shape (where k =1 in this case), λ was the wavelength of X-ray (where Cu Kα=0.154nm), β was the full width half maximum of the intensity (FWHM), and the θ was the corresponding Bragg angle. No characteristic peaks of other impurities are observed, indicating the high purity of the as-prepared samples. The narrow sharp peaks suggest that the Er/BiOBr products and pure BiOBr are highly crystalline.

For the study of energy level structure and chemical bonding, XPS measurements were carried out on 3wt% Er/BiOBr sample. The survey XPS spectroscopy (Fig. 2 a) shows that the 3wt% Er/BiOBr samples are composed of elements of Bi, O, Br, Er and C. The carbon peak comes from the adventitious carbon on the surface of the sample. In the high resolution spectrum (Fig. 2b), it can be seen that two strong peaks at 159.09eV and 164.50eV are assigned to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2, which is characteristic of Bi3+ in Er/BiOBr materials [27]. It can be found that there is a slight peak at 168.91eV (Fig. 2c), which can be assigned to Er 4d. It indicates that Er ions has been successfully doped into the samples [28]. The Br 3d (Fig. 2d) peak is associated with binding energy of 68.10eV which is characteristic of Br− in BiOBr materials [29]. Meanwhile, Fig. 2e shows that the peak binding energy of 529.90eV is assigned to O 1s [30]. As a result, it can be confirmed that the Er ions had been doped into the materials successfully.

In order to investigate the optical properties, the pure BiOBr and Er/BiOBr materials with different mass ratios were measured by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS), and the result is shown in Fig. 5 a. The absorption intensity of the as-prepared samples strengthened with increasing Er content (from 0wt% to 8wt%), which was in agreement with the color become darker. Compared with the pure BiOBr sample, Er/BiOBr samples exihibited apparently red shift, which means their enhanced light absorption. A classical Tauc method is employed to estimate the band gap energy (E g) of a semiconductor according to the following equation [31]: αE photon = K(E photon − E g) n
/2, where K is a constant, α is the absorption coefficient, E photon is the discrete photo energy and E g is the band gap energy. Among them, n depends on the characteristics of the transition in a semiconductor (direct transition n =1 and indirect transition n =4). For BiOBr, the value of n is 4 for the indirect transition. The energy intercept of a plot of (αE photon)1/2 vs. E photon yields E g for a direct transition. As is shown in Fig. 5b, 3wt% Er/BiOBr has narrower band gap of 2.56eV than the pure BiOBr of 2.66eV. It can be judged preliminary that Er/BiOBr composites had good response in the visible region to form more electron–hole pairs [32]. Then it can be further speculated that Er/BiOBr composites could have enhanced photocatalytic activity in visible light.

To further comprehend the process of electron transfer, the transient photocurrents of pure BiOBr and Er/BiOBr composites were measured on a photo-electrochemical test device. As is shown in Fig. 6 , the photocurrent increased sharply and then reached a stable value when the lamp was turned on. As soon as the lamp was turned off, the photocurrent rapidly decreased to initial status. In general, the higher the photocurrent was, the better the electron and hole separation efficiency would be, and thus the higher photocatalytic activity would be [33,34]. As is shown in the figure, the current intensity of 3wt% Er/BiOBr was 1.5 times of pure BiOBr, indicating that 3wt% Er/BiOBr exhibited higher separation efficiency of photoproduced electrons and holes. Therefore, 3wt% Er/BiOBr was expected to exhibit an enhanced photocatalytic activity by contrast with pure BiOBr illuminated under visible light.

The photocatalytic activities of pure and Er-doped BiOBr samples were evaluated by the degradation of CIP in water under visible light irradiation. CIP, widely used as sterilization drugs, could not be degraded in a state of nature, so the degradation of CIP is an extremely urgent matter to maintain ecosystem balance [35,36]. As shown in Fig. 7 , the Er/BiOBr materials exhibited enhanced photocatalytic activity than pure BiOBr, and the degradation efficiency of 3wt% Er/BiOBr reaches 61% compared to 50% for pure BiOBr sample after 6h irradiation. At the same time, the blank test proves the CIP is only slightly degraded in the absence of catalysts, indicating that the photolysis of CIP can be ignored. The temporal UV–vis absorption spectral changes during the photocatalytic degradation of CIP in aqueous solution in the presence of 3wt% Er/BiOBr materials is shown in Fig. 8 . As shown in Fig. 8, the absorption at λ =276nm of CIP evidently decreases with the increase of irradiation time and nearly disappears after 360min. It confirms the introduction of Er can improve the photocatalytic activity for the degradation of colorless model organic pollutant.

From the photocurrent response, it can be seen that 3wt% Er/BiOBr exhibits higher separation efficiency of electrons and holes than pure BiOBr, and the enhanced electron–hole pairs separation efficiency favor the improvement of photocatalytic activity under visible light irradiation. It could be found that the photocatalytic activity increased with increasing amount of doped Er ions until up to the optimum value and then decreased with the further doping. With the Er ions doped into BiOBr, the interfacial charge transfer can be improved for the Er ions located close to the surface of BiOBr, at the same time, the Er ions can trap electrons [37] effectively to abatement the recombination rate of photogenerated electron–hole pairs. The improved interfacial charge transfer and the high separation efficiency of electron–hole pairs can improve the photocatalytic activity. However, the excess ions doped into BiOBr can lessen the distance between trapping sites so that the trapped photogenerated electrons and holes can recombine lightly with the assistance of coulomb interaction [34]. On the basis of research in DRS, the band gap of the 3wt% Er/BiOBr has a narrower band gap of 2.56eV than the pure BiOBr sample, so it would be excited more easily by visible light and then enhance the photocatalytic activity. Therefore, the improved photocatalytic activity can be attributed to the synergistic effect of the above several factors. Based on the above analysis, the photocatalytic mechanism was proposed (Fig. 9 ). Under visible light irradiation, the BiOBr can be excited and engender photogenerated electron–hole pairs. Normally, the electron–hole pairs generated in the pure BiOBr sample would recombine rapidly. However, after the introduction of Er ions, the photogenerated electrons could be trapped by the Er ions and thus the recombination of electron–hole pairs could be restrained effectively. As a result, the Er/BiOBr materials have increased photocatalytic activity.

In summary, the Er/BiOBr composite photocatalysts have been successfully synthesized through a solvothermal process in the presence of [C16mim]Br. SEM and EDS mapping analysis showed that the formed sphere-like Er/BiOBr hierarchical structures are in an average diameter of 2–3μm and Er ions was successfully doped into BiOBr samples. The Er/BiOBr materials exhibited the enhanced photocatalytic activity than pure BiOBr for the degradation of CIP under visible light irradiation. The introduction of Er ions inhibited the recombination of photogenerated electron–hole pairs, which led to the enhancement of photocatalytic activity under visible light. This study suggested that Er/BiOBr composite photocatalysts would be a promising photocatalysts for the degradation of colorless model organic pollutant.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Nature Science Foundation of China (no. 21206060, 21476098 and 21471069), Jiangsu Province (1102118C), and the Special Financial Grant from the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2013T60506).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfa.2015.10.037.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:
